You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1. Pink 6x6 character at left edge of a dark room. Three 4x4 colored blocks on the right wall: red (top, row 19-22), blue (middle, row 25-28), green/N (bottom, row 31-34). A white tile path extends right from the character into the room. Bottom HUD bar shows order: pink, red, green, blue.
- Goal model: Visit/touch the colored blocks in the order shown on the bottom bar: red, then green, then blue.
- Action model: RIGHT/LEFT/UP/DOWN move the character. Need to probe movement.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to enter the room, then navigate to the red block first.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 109 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
print(f"Last action result: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact={last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

# Check character position after RIGHT
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Character: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: RIGHT changed the board but the character position is the same. So RIGHT doesn't move the character. Let me check what actually changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after RIGHT
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']
after = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']

def get_all_positions(nodes):
    pos = {}
    for n in nodes:
        if n['pixels'] > 10:  # Only significant objects
            rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            key = (n['color'], n['pixels'])
            if key not in pos:
                pos[key] = []
            pos[key].append((min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols)))
    return pos

before_pos = get_all_positions(before)
after_pos = get_all_positions(after)

print("Changes:")
all_keys = set(list(before_pos.keys()) + list(after_pos.keys()))
for k in sorted(all_keys):
    b = before_pos.get(k, 'N/A')
    a = after_pos.get(k, 'N/A')
    if b != a:
        print(f"  {k}: {b} -> {a}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
RIGHT changed something at row 53, which is in the HUD bar area. The gray bar at the bottom seems to shift. This is probably just a timer/progress indicator, not gameplay.
Let me reconsider the whole game. I've been making many actions without understanding the core mechanic. Let me think about what I know:
1. The character (pink M) is at the left side
2. There are 3 colored blocks on the right: red, blue, green
3. The bottom bar shows: pink, red, green, blue (the target order)
4. ACTION7 shifts everything up by 6 rows
5. DOWN shifts everything down by 6 rows (reverses ACTION7)
6. UP moves the character up (or shifts everything up?)
7. RIGHT changes something in the HUD bar
I've spent 109 actions and haven't made progress. Let me try a completely different approach. What if the game is about using MOUSE to click on the blocks? Or what if I need to arrange the blocks by clicking on them in a specific order?
Actually, let me look at the bottom bar more carefully. It shows: pink, red, green, blue. The pink is the character. The red, green, blue are the blocks in the desired order. Currently on the wall: red, blue, green. I need to rearrange them to red, green, blue.
What if I need to use MOUSE to click on the blocks to move them? Let me try clicking on the blue block to see what happens.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the ACTION7 mechanic. When I pressed ACTION7 while aligned with red (first time), red was "collected" (appeared on the bottom bar). But looking at the bottom bar now, red is already there. So maybe the bottom bar shows the ORDER in which blocks should be collected, and I need to collect them in that order.
But I've been pressing ACTION7 many times and only red was collected. The other times, ACTION7 just shifted everything up. So the key insight is: ACTION7 collects a block ONLY when the character is aligned with it AND it's the next block in the sequence.
The sequence is: red, green, blue. I've collected red. Now I need to collect green. But green is at rows 31-34 (or 37-40 depending on the current shift), and the character can only reach rows ~12-35.
Wait, let me check the current positions again and figure out how to align with green.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current positions
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('R', 'N', 'b', 'M') and n['pixels'] in (16, 18):
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"{n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Character at 24-29, blue at 25-28, green at 31-34
# To collect green, I need to align character with green
# Character is 6 rows tall (24-29), green is 4 rows (31-34)
# If I shift everything down by 6 (DOWN), character goes to 30-35, green goes to 37-40
# That doesn't help

# What if I shift everything up? ACTION7 shifts up by 6
# Character: 24-29 -> 18-23
# Blue: 25-28 -> 19-22
# Green: 31-34 -> 25-28
# Then character is at 18-23, green at 25-28. Still not aligned.

# What if I shift up more?
# ACTION7 again: character 12-17, blue 13-16, green 19-22
# Character at 12-17, green at 19-22. Still not aligned.

# ACTION7 again: character 6-11, blue 7-10, green 13-16
# Character at 6-11, green at 13-16. Still not aligned.

# It seems like the character and blocks maintain their relative positions when shifting
# The character is always 6 rows above blue, and blue is always 6 rows above green
# So the character can NEVER align with green by shifting!

# Unless... the character can move independently of the shift
# Let me check: when I pressed DOWN earlier, did ONLY the character move, or did everything move?

# From earlier data:
# Step 105: M=30-35, b=31-34, N=37-40
# Step 107 (after DOWN): M=30-35, b=31-34, N=37-40
# Wait, that's the same! So DOWN didn't change anything?

# Let me re-examine. After ACTION7 (step 106): M=24-29, b=25-28, N=31-34
# After DOWN (step 107): M=30-35, b=31-34, N=37-40
# So DOWN shifted EVERYTHING down by 6, including the blocks!

# This means the character and blocks always maintain the same relative positions
# The character is always overlapping with blue (since character is 6 rows tall and blue is 4 rows)
# So I can NEVER collect green or blue by shifting!

# Maybe the goal is different. Let me reconsider.
# What if I need to use MOUSE to click on the blocks?
print("\nTrying MOUSE on blue block...")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 26, 'col': 43}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
